data.title: Nike: It May Seem Like It, But The Drawdown Isn't Over, Tariffs Add Fuel To The Fire
data.description:
Introduction

I have been following Nike, Inc. (NYSE:NKE) stock for a few years now. For a few weeks, I've been following the recent drawdown very closely, as I didn't think we would see these prices again. After Nike shares dropped below $70, I thought the stock should be in buy territory, so I looked further into its fundamentals, especially the recent drawdown, which is the second largest and most severe since Nike's IPO in 1980. Only two drawdowns come close in terms of both percentage loss and duration.

However, while these drawdowns seem similar to the current one, there are two big differences. In the following article, I will focus on comparing Nike's current drawdown to similar drawdowns and show that there are two significant differences that you should know before making an investment decision.I will also show the countries and tariffs that matter to Nike stock and explain why Nike is falling so drastically after these tariffs.

Overview of recent events

If you follow Nike's stock, you will probably know this, but I still want to explain the recent events and developments that led to the downfall of Nike's stock.

Nike loses market share to other brands like Hoka, On, and Adidas

In 2024, Nike lost market share to brands like On (ONON), Hoka (DECK), and Adidas (OTCQX:ADDYY), especially in the running shoe sector. GlobalData has researched this and presents these changes in 2024:

As you can see, Nike lost ground compared to 2023. Also worth mentioning is that every other brand except Nike and Puma (OTCPK:PMMAF) has increased their market share. Not a good look for Nike and Puma. But, on a positive note, Nike is still far ahead of its competition when it comes to market share.

According to sources like Brand Z and Brand Finance, Nike is still #1, but is losing “coolness” among teenagers. (source)

Financials have been struggling since 2023

In 2023 (calendar year, not Nike's fiscal year), Nike's fundamentals started to crack. While revenue was still up in 2023 (+10%), earnings dropped by 16%. Rising revenue and falling earnings are always a warning sign for me. The earnings drop came due to high inventories that had to be worked down with discounts. This pressured margins.

In 2024, net EPS had a small comeback before decreasing again. Free cash flow was higher than in 2023, but fell at the end of the year. Revenue, however, couldn't keep up the growth rates of 2023 and even declined.

Margins have declined too, especially in 2024:

Nike's inventory, which created problems in 2023, is still elevated, but not as much as in 2022. So the situation is a bit better here. But the inventory days outstanding (DOI) are still elevated, meaning that inventory is still taking longer to be moved. Hence, the inventory turnover rate is lower.

Higher costs and interest rates

Since 2022, almost every company has seen cost pressures, higher logistics costs, and higher interest rates. Nike is no exception here. According to management, especially higher labor and freight costs have put pressure on earnings.

New tariffs on Vietnam, Indonesia, and China

Just yesterday evening, President Trump announced the new tariffs. The tariffs include a 46% levy on imports from Vietnam, 32% on Indonesia, and an additional 34% on Chinese goods. Nike's supply chain is heavily dependent on these three countries, which is probably the reason why Nike is 11% in the red after tariffs.

Nike's reliance on these countries for production is considerable:​

  • Vietnam: Approximately 50% of Nike's footwear and 28% of its apparel are manufactured in Vietnam

  • Indonesia: Accounts for 27% of Nike's footwear production

  • China: Responsible for 18% of footwear and 16% of apparel production

This will have several adverse effects on Nike:

  • Reduced profit margins: Higher tariffs directly increase the cost of goods, reducing Nike's profit margins unless these costs can be passed on to the consumer.

  • Potential price increases: To maintain profitability, Nike has to pass on the increased costs to consumers, leading to higher prices for its products. This could result in decreased consumer demand.

  • Earnings impact: Analysts predict that these significant tariffs could reduce sector margins by over 10 percentage points.

Later on, I will explain that this is not the only pressure on Nike's sales, margins, and earnings.

Outlook

Note: The new tariffs are not yet reflected in these forecasts.

For the next quarter, management expects a revenue decline of approximately 15%. For the full year, management expects revenue declines in the mid-single to double-digit percentage range.

This is, again, due to inventory challenges. Inventory challenges have probably been pressuring revenue and earnings for several quarters. Furthermore, management names tariffs, geopolitical tensions, and currency changes as additional headwinds. As you can see in the following chart, the next-twelve-months (NTM) EPS and revenue consensus average for Nike have fallen since March 2024. After the recent earnings report, expectations by analysts fell again.

Revenue expectations are back to the levels of 2021. EPS expectations are at the level of 2014, over ten years ago—down by almost 65% from its high of $4.3 in 2021.

Considering this, it is not surprising that Nike is down big from its former high:

Nike has lost over 60% from its high in 2021 and is currently on par with its low from December 2018, and almost as low as at the COVID-19 low in March 2020. One more bad day, and the share price will reach the highs of 2015 again. This would eliminate 10 years of gains.

In comments here on Seeking Alpha, at my job as a securities specialist, and also in my circle of friends and family, I have heard several times that Nike must be a buy again now after it has fallen this much and is near COVID lows. Looking at the drawdown and comparing it with the only two more severe drawdowns, I have to say otherwise.

Let's compare Nike's drawdowns

The only more severe drawdown has been in 1984. The drawdown in the year 2000 comes close.

After looking at this, I understand that people could think that everything negative should be priced in by now. But let me show you two big differences between the drawdowns from 1984, 2000, and now.

1. Difference: Valuations

Both in 1984 and in 2000, at the peak of the drawdown, Nike's valuation was not only less than it is now, but it also fell significantly.

In 1984, Nike traded at 6.5x P/E, down from 20.2x at the beginning of the drawdown.

In 2000, Nike's P/E was 14.6x at the peak of the drawdown, down from 26.2x at the beginning of the drawdown and 73x from its high in 1999.

At both times, Nike's P/E was below the average of the five years before the drawdown.

Right now, Nike trades at a P/E of 21x. This is down from 46x at the last all-time high:

A difference is that this time, the peak of the drawdown does not represent the lowest P/E.

A bit more significant in my eyes is the 12-month forward P/E. I like to use this much more because we are investing in the future of a company, not its past. Therefore, I think the forward P/E gives much better information.

Unfortunately, there is no data on the forward P/E in 1984, so we can't compare that. But there is for the year 2000:

At the lowest share price in 2000, Nike traded at a fwd P/E of 12.2x, far below the times before.

Nowadays, Nike trades at a fwd P/E of 35.8x. This is not only well above the 12.2x from 2000 but also well above its average fwd P/E of 30.4x.

This shows that even though Nike's share price has dropped significantly, the shares are still more expensive than on average since the drawdown started, and much more expensive than in 2000. Considering the problems Nike is in right now, we haven't seen nearly enough valuation compression yet, despite the significant drop in share price. This becomes even clearer when comparing the current data to 1984 and 2000.

And before you want to argue that Nike is a much more mature company now than it was in 1984 or 2000, and that valuations tend to be higher nowadays anyway, let me show you the second big difference.

2. Difference: revenue and earnings were in a better state both in 1984 and the outlook was better in 2000

This is how revenue, EPS and free cash flow looked in 1981-1986:

I don't have the quarterly data, but I think the yearly data is enough to see that revenue continued to grow while EPS dropped significantly. Free cash flow dropped too and became negative. The EPS and FCF development was bad; revenue, however, continued to grow.

In the year 2000, revenue, EPS, and FCF fell too. Notably, Nike started gaining after the worst in its financials had been seen.

Another important difference is, that in the time of the drawdowns peak the analyst forecast looked way better than it does now:

The forecast was mostly steady, rather positive at the peak of the drawdown. Right after, the analysts upped their expectations.

As shown here, expectations are in free fall right now.

What I want to show here is that in the two former drawdowns, revenue and EPS fell too. However, at least in 2000, the forecast was much better. Furthermore, revenue and EPS declines had reached their lowest point before the peak of the drawdown. Considering that revenue and EPS will continue to fall, I would argue that the bottom may not be in.

Market uncertainties and a cracking U.S. consumer

Note: All of this data was collected/presented before the tariff announcement yesterday. So it is to be expected that this data will worsen even more in the coming days or weeks.

Right now, the market is under pressure from uncertainty due to Trump's tariffs and other headlines that shake up the market.

According to the University of Michigan, 51% of the people in the U.S. think the current news is negative. This is by far the highest it has been since the beginning of this statistic.

Since tariffs will increase prices, inflation expectations have risen sharply:

The University of Michigan projects inflation to be at 5% in a year from now. This brings inflation expectations back to the levels of July 2022.

The earnings growth expectations have also fallen sharply for the S&P 500 (SP500) since the beginning of the year:

Considering this data, the Atlanta Fed has dramatically reduced the GDP growth outlook from +3.5% to -1.5% for Q1 2025. Overall, even Q4 2025 won't reach the highs of 2024 again, meaning that 2025 will be a year of negative GDP growth.

After seeing this, it is not surprising that the percentage of people expecting stocks to rise in the next twelve months has come drastically:

The bigger problem for Nike's sales and earnings will probably be the deteriorating U.S. consumer confidence.

As you can see in the chart, U.S. consumer confidence has dropped significantly and currently sits at a point only seen five other times since 1955. For reference: anything above 75 is considered a good and positive sentiment.

Other data regarding the U.S. consumer doesn't look much better either.

MoM Personal Spending in the U.S. for January not only fell the most since January 2023 but also blew past the consensus. From +0.8% to -0.2% while the consensus estimate of 0.1% is alarming.

Here you can see the specific changes in consumer spending. What catches the eye is that almost everything that saw higher consumer spending is a necessity, something consumers can't stop spending money on. Motor vehicles and parts were hit the most.

Another metric that makes consumer spending look bad is the personal savings rate:

While the personal income grew MoM, the personal savings rate grew even more from 3.5% to 4.6% in January 2025. This shows that the US consumer is not in the mood to spend money. Not even when personal income grows.

Nike is one of the companies being the most dependent on the US consumer. So these changes are significant for the stock.

Conclusion

Even though Nike's share price of now under $60 could seem cheap compared to its highs from three years ago—especially when you look at the drawdown and see that the stock is down more than during the Great Financial Crisis—I think the charts made it clear that we probably haven't seen the bottom yet, considering that revenue and EPS will continue to decline according to analysts and management itself. The decline will be worsened by the newly announced tariffs from yesterday evening. Nike's competition — namely On Holding, Lululemon Athletica (LULU), and Adidas — gained market share and took it mostly from Nike. According to Brand Z, Nike lost “coolness” in the teenage customer segment.

Furthermore, the EPS consensus for the next twelve months declined even more than the share price, bringing the fwd P/E up significantly and above the mean of recent years. So even though the share price has declined dramatically, the stock isn't cheap. At this point, Nike's shares could be a turnaround play, but for this to happen, there needs to be some P/E compression.

Under the current market uncertainties around tariffs, I think it is hard to give a price target. But I definitely think that Nike is about to fall further. I would advise waiting to start a position unless the fundamentals have bottomed and consumer sentiment is improving.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: TCW Compounders ETF Q4 2024 Commentary
data.description:
Investment Thesis

GRW is an actively managed fund focused on companies with market leadership positions and proven track records of resilient, predictable growth and significant free cash flow generation. Our thesis is that investing in businesses with durable competitive advantages, essential or hard-to-replicate products or services and exceptionally skilled management teams will drive significant compounding performance over the long term. Through GRW, investors can invest in and benefit from the robust performance generated by these exceptionally high-quality companies.

Performance

Net of fees, GRW delivered a return of -1.5% in Q4 2024, compared to the S&P 500 Index’s return of 2.4% and returns for the Russell 1000 Index of 2.7%. For the year ended December 31, 2024, GRW returned 22.6%, while the S&P 500 Index and Russell 1000 Index returned 25.0% and 24.5%, respectively. Since its inception in January 2016, GRW has delivered a cumulative return of 346.6%. This compares to 255.5% for the S&P 500 Index (SP500)(SPX) and 251.5% for the Russell 1000 Index over the same period. 1

Our investments in Fiserv (FI) and Visa (V), both key businesses in the payments value chain were our largest contributors in the quarter. Both companies benefitted from increased optimism in a more favorable policy backdrop and the potential for deregulation spurring greater business activity from large enterprises to SMBs. Fiserv shares were driven by a solid third quarter earnings report in which revenues were in line with expectations but a 70 bp margin beat led to earnings per share that were $0.04 above consensus estimates. Management also slightly raised 2024 organic revenue and free cash flow guidance. Finally, the company executed $1.2 billion in Q3 share buybacks, bringing year-to-date repurchases to ~$4.5 billion. Visa shares surged after the company reported third quarter revenues and earnings that beat expectations, driven by a 13% increase in cross-border volumes. The company continues to benefit from a durable consumer spending backdrop.

Headwinds to performance during the quarter were dispersed between several investments. Constellation Software shares traded down 5.2% on no company-specific news. HEICO (HEI) shares fell late in the quarter after reporting sales that were slightly weaker than anticipated, mostly due to softness from the company’s Electronic Technologies Group (ETG). We expect that the challenges to ETG from inventory destocking are transitory and should begin to alleviate in the coming year. We also expect continued robust performance from the Flight Support Group supported by after-market commercial aviation demand and the company’s strong market position with lower cost FAA approved parts.

Linde (LIN) shares were down -11.9% in the quarter in-line with the Materials Select Sector Spider (XLB). There was no news to attribute the share price reaction to. We are optimistic about the company’s robust >$10 billion backlog and its ability to capture price should inflation remain stickier than hoped.

Market Commentary

The U.S. stock market, as represented by the S&P 500 Index, delivered an impressive return of 25.0% (total return) through 2024. This positive performance was driven by corporate earnings that have exceeded market expectations and a solid macroeconomic backdrop that seemed to support the Fed’s goal to achieve a “soft landing”.

With fourth quarter earnings season kicking off, the S&P 500 is forecasted to see annual EPS growth of nearly 11% in 2024 (UBS, 01/06/2025), although the top-heavy concentration of the index certainly inflates that figure. The “Magnificent 7”, whose market capitalization consistently made up 30% of the S&P 500 in 2024, have buoyed EPS growth, with 2024 estimates at 36.2%, and the rest of the companies in the index (the S&P 493), with a much more modest 4.8% estimate.

Looking forward, earnings growth is expected to accelerate in 2025, while dispersion is expected to improve. In 2025, earnings for the S&P 500, Magnificent 7, and S&P 493 are projected to be 12.6%, 19.7%, and 9.2%, respectively, which we believe to be an attractive set up for us as active investors.

At a macro level, the economy appears to be on solid footing, although this has led to a sharp change in expectations for the Fed to continue its cut cycle. The unemployment rate is still historically low (4.1% in December), while average hourly earnings growth is above the historical average of the last two decades (3.9% in December). At the same time, the Fed’s progress on inflation has seemingly stalled (2.9% in December), and expectations for interest cuts in 2025 fell from ~5 cuts of ~120 bps at the start of the quarter, to ~4 cuts of ~43 bps through the end of the quarter (Bloomberg, 01/21/2025).

Finally, the Trump 2.0 administration is a major question mark for the U.S. and global economies. So far, the U.S. equity market has strongly approved of his victory while international markets have provided a more mixed response. Trump is notoriously tough on China and Mexico, and generally champions any movement that puts “America first”, however, his comments have historically often lacked follow-through. Trump and his cohorts have promised to cut regulations that are seen as “bad for [U.S.] business”, which is perhaps one of the main contributors to post-election performance.

In the present environment, we believe it remains prudent to focus on compelling secular trends which are more insulated from economic growth worries. As always, we reiterate our unswerving focus on all-weather businesses which exhibit proven management skill, pricing power, low levels of indebtedness, strong free cash flow generation, and a likely resilience in the face of a pronounced economic slowdown.

1. Effective January 2, 2025 the Fund’s benchmark changed to the S&P 500 Index. On this date, the use of the Russell 1000® Index as a benchmark for this Fund was discontinued.

For standardized performance and current holdings please visit etf.tcw.com/grw/

Important Information

Before investing you should carefully consider the fund’s investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses. This and other information is in the prospectus, a copy of which may be obtained from etf.tcw.com. Please read the prospectus carefully before you invest.

As with any investment, you could lose all or part of your investment in the Fund, and the Fund’s performance could trail that of other investments. The Fund is subject to certain risks, including the principal risks noted below, any of which may adversely affect the Fund’s net asset value (“NAV”) per share, trading price, yield, total return and ability to meet its investment objective.

The description of TCW ETF’s investment strategy is intended to be representative but may be changed from time to time by TCW, and TCW may alter the information at its discretion. TCW intends to be focused and directed in the selection of opportunities to actively engage with portfolio companies of the Fund. As we seek to deliver on our client’s financial objectives, engagement and active ownership are integral components of TCW’s research and investment process. Our data-informed engagement and active ownership practices achieve several objectives. The information elicited from these practices not only helps improve our fundamental research, but our engagement and active ownership practices may also have positive impacts on the company or other entities by suggesting best practices in addressing critical, financially material issues in areas of sustainability, corporate governance, or executive compensation. In TCW’s view, active ownership improves value and informs future investment decisions.

Forward-looking statements are subject to various risks and uncertainties. Accordingly, there are or will be important factors that could cause actual outcomes or results to differ materially from those indicated in these statements. TCW undertakes no obligation to publicly update or review any forward-looking statement, whether as a result of new information, future developments or otherwise. Portfolio holdings included in this presentation excludes cash.

DEFINED TERMS

CPI (Consumer Price Index) – Measures the average change in prices over time that consumers pay for a basket of goods and services.

Free Cash Flow (FCF) – FCF represents the cash that a company is able to generate after spending the money required to maintain or expand its asset base.

Magnificent 7 – The Magnificent Seven stocks are a group of high-performing and influential companies in the U.S. stock market: Alphabet, Amazon, Apple, Meta

Platforms, Microsoft, NVIDIA, and Tesla.

INVESTMENT RISKS

TCW Compounders ETF (NYSE:GRW) seeks to invest in the companies that the Adviser believes will benefit from transformation as a result of technological innovations, market dynamics, and/or changes in client preferences. The Fund’s investment objective is long-term growth of capital. The Funds may purchase and write put and call options on futures contracts that are traded on an exchange as a hedge against changes in value of its portfolio securities, or in anticipation of the purchase of securities, and may enter into closing transactions with respect to such options to terminate existing positions. There is no guarantee that such closing transactions can be effected. Undervalued stocks may not realize their perceived value for extended periods of time or may never realize their perceived value. Source: FactSet as of 12/31/2024. Past performance is not a reliable indicator or guarantee of future results. Due to market volatility, the market may not perform in a similar manner in the future. Indexes are unmanaged, do not reflect the deduction of fees or expenses, and are not available for direct investment. The index data provided is not representative of any Lord Abbett product.

Market Review

  • The municipal market, as represented by the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, returned -1.22% during the fourth quarter of 2024. Over the calendar year, the index returned 1.05%. In terms of sector, the Electric and Special Tax sectors outperformed while the longer duration Housing sector lagged.[1] Within investment grade municipals, AA-rated bonds outperformed, while AAA-rated and BBB-rated bonds trailed. High yield municipal bonds, as represented by the Bloomberg High Yield Municipal Bond Index, outperformed investment grade bonds, and returned -1.08% in the fourth quarter, bringing calendar year performance to 6.32%.[1]
  • Over the quarter, yields[2] moved higher, and the curve flattened with short-term rates rising most. The 10-year to 30- year segment continues to show considerable steepness, ending the quarter with a slope of 84 basis points (bps), which compares to a slope of just 21 bps for the Treasury curve over the same range.
  • According to Lipper data[3], municipal bond funds experienced strong demand in October and November while experiencing outflows in December amid rate volatility and year-end tax trading. Over the calendar year, there were approximately $41 billion of inflows in municipal funds, with Long-Term Funds leading all categories.
  • Total municipal issuance continued to come in at elevated levels in the fourth quarter, totaling $122 billion for the period. Over the calendar year, total municipal issuance reached a record high of $504 billion, driven by a 41% increase in the supply of tax-exempt bonds compared to the trailing 5-year average.[3]

Portfolio Review

  • The Fund returned -1.05%, reflecting performance at the net asset value (NAV) of class I Shares with all distributions reinvested, for the quarter ended December 31, 2024. The Fund’s secondary benchmark, a 65%/35% blend[4] of the Bloomberg High Yield Municipal Bond Index and the Bloomberg Municipal Bond Index, returned -1.13% during the same period.
  • Security selection was the primary contributor to relative performance, mainly within the Health Care and Industrial Development sectors. Within the Health Care sector, selection of lower quality hospital bonds traded higher and contributed to relative performance.
  • An overweight allocation to the Industrial Development and Transportation sectors led to a negative impact. We remain positive on the IDR sector. Although corporate borrowing costs have increased and corporations have seen some adverse impacts from inflation, many corporate-backed municipal issuers tend to provide essential services or produce materials critical to infrastructure, energy procurement and global stability.
  • The Fund’s longer duration positioning was the primary detractor from relative performance, as municipal yields rose across the curve on the back of increasing long-term Treasury yields.

Strategy Positioning And Outlook

  • We continue to be underweight callable bonds that are priced close to par to improve the performance characteristics of the Fund and positioning the portfolio to potentially have higher probabilities of performing better in a wide range of potential interest rate scenarios. Along the same lines, we favor bonds with higher coupons and longer calls along with bonds trading at deep discounts to position in more favorable structures.
  • With the municipal yield curve showing considerable steepness from 10 to 30 years, we continue to focus on the most attractive segments of the curve from a total return and roll down perspective, while selling flatter segments of the curve or maturities that do not provide much incremental yield for added risk.
  • Although primarily invested in non-investment grade bonds, the Fund has an allocation to ‘A’ and ‘BBB’ rated bonds to provide diversification and enhance liquidity. We also believe that lower-rated investment grade bonds are showing attractive relative value in the current market environment.
  • As of the close of 2024, municipal yields across the curve are higher than at the start of the year and remain near their highest levels of the past decade. We believe the income available in today’s environment presents a potentially attractive total return opportunity for investors regardless of how prospective interest rate cuts by the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) impact overall rates going into the new year.
  • Looking at technical factors, supply of municipal new issues increased significantly during 2024 and closed the year at record highs, as issuers hastened to bring deals to market before the November election, among other factors. We expect supply to remain above average in the new year. However, we believe the excess supply will continue to be absorbed by strong demand from market participants.
  • Strong demand is apparent in examining flows into municipal bond mutual funds, which closed calendar year 2024 with over $40 billion in inflows. Additionally, flows into separately managed account products remained strong all year. Given attractive tax-exempt yields, record levels of cash “on the sidelines” (i.e., held in instruments such as U.S. Treasury bills and money market funds), and the start of a new Fed rate-cut cycle (the last six of which have led to sustained periods of inflows), we expect demand for municipal bonds to remain strong and likely increase in the new year.
  • Credit quality for the overall municipal bond market remains healthy, in our view, and we believe it will continue to be supported by a relatively strong economy. In terms of ratings actions, upgrades continued to outpace downgrades over the previous year, according to S&P Global. And while tax receipts have moderated from the record-setting years coming out of the pandemic, tax revenues experienced growth in 2024, and U.S. state governments' "rainy day" balances remain near record highs.
  • The political environment brings some uncertainty for the market in 2025. The incoming U.S. administration has expressed a desire to lower taxes, which could result in a larger U.S. budget deficit and some market volatility. When income tax rates move lower, speculation typically turns to how the government might find other sources of revenue. One of the things that may come up in those conversations is the tax exemption of municipal bonds. We don't believe that the tax exemption of municipals will be eliminated or significantly changed. However, we will be keeping an eye on related policy discussions that could affect the municipal bond market, although we think these impacts will be minor.

data.title: JPMorgan Chase: Tariffs Loom Over This Top Bank Ahead Of Q1 Earnings
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase & Co. (NYSE:JPM) is one of the best banks in the industry. It is well diversified, and even with different focuses it manages to be very efficient. Now, let me tell you some things you might not already know!

The company's ROE (LTM), as shown in the chart below, was 17.4%, while peers such as Bank of America Corporation (BAC), The Goldman Sachs Group, Inc. (GS), and Morgan Stanley (MS), were below 14%. That's a big spread in a super-competitive industry.

Now, that comparison is a bit tilted in favor of JPM to begin with, since it has exposure through 3 main divisions: Consumer Banking, Commercial & Investment Banking, and Asset Management. But the flip side of that argument is that this is precisely why this "bank stock" is treated royally by so many. I am old enough to remember when Jamie Dimon was the heir apparent, not the long-tenured leader of JPM, whose stature grows every year. Heck, he's been discussed in some off-the-radar circles as a potential US Presidential candidate. Or at least the Treasury Secretary.

The bank's merit is clear, as JPM manages to have an optimized structure, allowing for those excellent returns. Over the years, we can also see that the strategy has been efficient on multiple fronts, with significant gains in market share and very important operational advances, such as going from a 7.5% market share in U.S. Retail deposits in 2013 to more than 11% in 2023.

This solidity, excellent track record, and ongoing initiatives for expansion and modernization allow us to envision JPM maintaining its good indicators, but is this enough to own JPM stock?

Valuation Is Not That Good

JPMorgan is expected to report Q1 earnings next week, and the market estimates an EPS of $4.58, a YoY growth of 3%. If, on the one hand, these expectations make it easier for the bank to beat earnings estimates, on the other it shows us that the market is not so optimistic about the coming quarters. The environment is complex to operate in, with a lot of uncertainty both at a national level (with tariffs and the like) and at a global level, which affects the economy and the bank's activities.

Another point that goes beyond short-term expectations is that even in this scenario, JPM's valuation is nothing great, just ordinary. The shareholder yield (buyback yield + dividend yield) is 4.9%, fine compared to the rest of the market right now (but I can't eat relative returns, right?), but substantially below its own historical average and that of peers such as GS and BAC.

The price-to-book is also above its peers, but to reward the best ROE, it was to be expected. Despite this, when compared to its historical average, the P/B is also high, exceeding 2x, while the mean of the last 10 years is 1.5x.

With cloudy expectations and a valuation above the historical average, there aren't many good reasons to buy JPM stock before Q1 earnings, giving it a hold for now. And while the tariff drama of this week (and likely beyond) will likely impact other sectors and industries more than this one, a reverse of the giant wealth effect we've seen build-up, which benefited JPM greatly, could put in the same line to be sold off with the rest of the stock market. Still, that could be temporary.

JPM earnings: what a time to go first!

JPM has the pleasure (he wrote wryly) of headlining the start of quarterly earnings next Friday morning, April 11. As shown here, 10 upward revisions from Wall Street analysts against zero downgrades would seem to bode well.

However, this is 2025, where anything can happen, and usually does. So again, the market reaction more so than the actual numbers and forecast from this pivotal market leader might be the weather vane for what we'll hear during the rest of April and May as the rest of the S&P 500 reports.

Dividend status: solid

JPM's dividend is only 2.3%, so while the steady growth and long-term dividend-paying ability are just fine for now, and the payout ratio reasonable, the dividend might be less of an attraction if the stock and the broad market sell-off. To me, a dividend is great, but only to the extent it represents a return above a zero price gain. In other words, a 2.3% dividend but 10-40% price risk is not a balancing act I try to court.

What does the chart say?

I'll say this: I've seen many worse charts among US blue-chip stocks in 2025. However, the issue here is now not contained by JPM's accolades, as noted above.

At a market cap now more than $650 billion, JPM, the 12th largest stock in the S&P 500, is right in the throes of whatever reverse-indexation action continues to creep into the market. That is, all of that S&P 500 index money that investors have piled in over the past decade: if the tariffs prompt selling, emotionally or otherwise, the 12th most sold stock in terms of dollar value will be JPM. That's something that has been front of mind for me as I've witnessed the historic accumulation of assets into such funds.

JPM is also the second-largest holding in the financial sector, as reflected by its 10% weighting in the $51 billion in assets in The Financial Select Sector SPDR® Fund ETF (XLF).

So, there is more at play here than traditional fundamental factors. Money flows matter, now more than ever. JPM is as solid as we'll find in a bank or financial stock. However, that's a company, a business. What stocks do can be determined by additional factors, which are the reality of modern markets.

Finally, as I continue to gradually introduce ROAR (Reward Opportunity And Risk), my proprietary price-based system that assigns a probability of the next 10% move in a stock or ETF being up and not down, consider this:

This is as of Tuesday's close, and this is one of those very rare moments when traditional metrics might be very helpful or completely irrelevant for a period of time. So with JPM closing Tuesday at a 34, that implies a 1 in 3 chance that it will regain most of its recent decline (to around $270 a share), and a 2 out of 3 chance that its next double-digit percentage move will be down toward $220, an area it last travelled through about 5 months ago.

This is a market that is tilted to the downside in a big way. But there's always a chance. So we'll give JPM some benefit of the doubt, owing to its strong franchise. More significantly, our penchant for taking ratings in a very different manner than many on this platform (we are more concerned with allocation size and whether a position is hedged or not, versus the "binary" buy/sell approach) leads us to hold rating on a relative basis to the masses of very weak-looking stocks that surround JPM in the large-cap US stock market.

data.title: Editas Medicine: Do The Risks Outweigh The Rewards?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Editas Medicine (NASDAQ:EDIT) has been advanced by some as a promising CRISPR gene-editing play, especially after a recent article highlighted its pivot to in vivo therapies and cash runway. However, we see numerous challenges and uncertainties that cause us to take a more bearish stance on EDIT. We believe the company’s pipeline lacks meaningful supporting data to warrant an investment in its stock. Also, its timelines for in vivo breakthroughs appear to us as overly optimistic, which makes us question the actual strength of its cash position. When comparing Editas to peers like Intellia (NTLA) and Beam (BEAM), the optimism appears to be overstated.

Strategic pivots and past missteps further erode our confidence in EDIT. The stock has declined more than 80% from its 2024 highs. For the reasons we will discuss, we are not investing in Editas given its uncertain pipeline, limited cash, absence of clear near-term catalysts, and substantial downside risks.

Pipeline with Limited Supporting Data

Editas’s clinical pipeline has produced sparse data so far, and what data exists gives us concerns. The company’s initial flagship program, EDIT-101, for a rare blindness disorder (LCA10), was the first-ever in vivo CRISPR therapy tested in humans. In our opinion, the outcomes from that trial were disappointing. In the Phase 1/2 BRILLIANCE trial, only three out of 14 patients showed a meaningful improvement in vision​.

The responders were confined to a tiny genetic subpopulation (roughly 300 patients in the US), prompting Editas to pause the trial and seek a partner rather than forge ahead alone​. This effectively shelved what was once a lead program due to a lack of efficacy and commercial viability. In our opinion, this is a significant setback, and it highlights a troubling pattern: Editas has yet to deliver convincing clinical results in any indication.

Beyond the eye trial, we believe the company’s disclosures on other programs have been limited. Editas did advance an ex vivo gene-edited cell therapy for sickle cell disease and beta thalassemia (known as reni-cel, formerly EDIT-301) into clinical trials, but subsequently decided to discontinue reni-cel altogether after failing to secure a commercial partner​. This means Editas has now abandoned its only late-stage asset, which, in our view, is a clear sign that its leadership saw diminishing value in continuing.

At this point, Editas is pivoting entirely to preclinical in vivo programs. This appears to leave the company with no active clinical-stage products. The pipeline’s promise is now based on preclinical findings in animals without human data. In our view, there is little concrete data from such findings to validate the bullish thesis. Until the company can show real, reproducible data in patients, we remain cautious about the company's long-term prospects.

Ambitious Timelines

The bullish case for Editas seems to be based on its strategic transition to in vivo gene editing. In December 2024, Editas announced it aims to achieve human proof-of-concept for an in vivo CRISPR therapy within approximately two years. We believe this aggressive timeline warrants skepticism. Editas' attempt to deliver gene editors inside patients to modify cells directly in the body is an exciting, but challenging objective. To date, our research has led us to conclude that the only company that has shown clear success in doing this in humans is NTLA. NTLA's 2021 study delivered CRISPR to the liver and achieved around 87–93% reduction of a disease-causing protein with a single dose​.

Yet, even with that success, NTLA's program (for transthyretin amyloidosis) is still in clinical trials and not yet approved. Editas now wants to apply in vivo editing to even more challenging tissues like hematopoietic stem cells in bone marrow. While the company claims to have a novel delivery nanoparticle that achieved roughly 40% editing of a target gene in a humanized mouse model, translating this to human patients is a giant leap. After all, preclinical proof-of-concept in mice or monkeys is no guarantee of safety or efficacy in humans. Many promising therapies have failed when making the jump to human clinical trials.

In our view, the timeline of approximately two years to get human proof-of-concept may be overly optimistic. Our research indicates that as of early 2025, Editas has not even begun a clinical trial for an in vivo therapy. It will likely take most of 2025 for the company to select development candidate, clear IND-enabling studies, and get FDA's permission to start a trial. Assuming a first-in-human trial starts in 2026, expecting meaningful proof-of-concept data before 2027 is, in our opinion, ambitious.

The bullish thesis on Editas overlooks the considerable scientific and regulatory hurdles ahead. We believe the most likely scenario is that development takes longer and faces some setbacks. We view the company's published timelines to be viewed as best-case scenarios.

Financial Challenges

As of December 31, 2024, Editas reported $322 million in cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities. The company stated that this will fund operations into Q2 2027. However, we believe this projection assumes the company can stick to its current cash-burn rate after cutting its workforce by approximately 65% and discontinuing reni-cel.

We question whether the current runway allows for multiple clinical trials or scaling manufacturing capabilities. And given the absence of any active human studies and the capital-intensive nature of gene editing, we find it hard to see Editas maintaining its preclinical objectives without raising capital before 2027. We believe this is especially true if early in vivo efforts show promise and require acceleration, or if further hiccups require more strategic pivoting akin to what happened with reni-cel.

Comparing Editas to Peers Like Intellia and Beam

The bullish position may favorably compare Editas to some of its gene-editing peers. While it is true that all these players share the CRISPR gene-editing theme, we believe the differences are critical and mostly to Editas’s detriment. In our opinion, Editas is not Intellia or Beam, and drawing direct comparisons overstates the current value of Editas.

As an example, NTLA has, in our opinion, earned a $755 million market cap because it has shown Wall Street real progress by delivering the first-ever systemic CRISPR treatment in humans with dramatic results (over 80% to 90% knockdown of a disease protein). NTLA now has at least two in vivo programs in clinical trials (for ATTR amyloidosis and hereditary angioedema). Both programs have yielded positive early data. NTLA also benefits from a pipeline of additional candidates.

By contrast, Editas has no in vivo programs in the clinic to date. Based on the latest earnings release, the earliest IND submission is targeted for mid-2026, putting human trials at least a year or more away. In our view, NTLA's higher valuation is justified by its head start, validated delivery platform, and clinical data. Editas’s lower valuation, on the other hand, reflects its pivot, recent pipeline reset, and lack of human proof-of-concept.

Similarly, BEAM comes up as a peer to Editas. Beam specializes in base editing, among other forms of editing. The company has shown that a single dose of its in vivo candidate for AATD can push AAT levels over the protective threshold, while simultaneously knocking down the toxic Z-protein by nearly 80%. These are promising results.

The same goes for BEAM-101 in sickle cell. Patients are showing elevated fetal hemoglobin (HbF) levels over 60%—well above what’s seen in historical controls—and a corresponding drop in sickle hemoglobin. There were no significant safety issues either.

Crucially, Beam has attracted significant validation from larger players – notably, a $300 million upfront partnership with Pfizer in 2022​ to collaborate on base-edited therapies for rare diseases. That massive deal not only boosted Beam’s cash reserves, but also signaled that big pharma sees enough promise in Beam’s technology to invest heavily. In fact, we see Beam's partnership with Pfizer as a potential pathway for the latter to buy Beam outright if the collaboration truly yields strong results. Editas, on the other hand, has no such big pharma partnerships and none that appear to be on the horizon.

Conclusion: Downside Risks Outweigh the Upside

We believe the benefits of an investment in Editas today are outweighed by its risks. First, the company has essentially no clinically proven product to date. Past programs delivered lukewarm results or were discontinued, which, in our opinion, underscores poor execution. While the new pipeline may be encouraging, it remains unvalidated, and the company's history of poor execution concerns us that it will spill over into its in vivo plans.

Second, we believe investors in Editas will have to wait until 2026 or potentially later for a significant catalyst that could alleviate the latest downside pressure in stock price. With the reset to in vivo work, there likely will not be any significant clinical readouts until 2026 or later. Aside from periodic updates or possibly a small preclinical data readout in 2025, we are unaware of any catalysts to drive the stock upward in the near term.

Third, while the company has not indicated that it will return to the market for a dilutive capital raise, we remain concerned that it may end up having a limited cash runway relative to its objectives. In our opinion, the assessed runway to 2027 ​could be revised if hiccups or additional needs accelerate cash burn.

Our Bottom-Line

We believe the risks of investing in Editas today outweigh the potential rewards. In our opinion, the company has no validated products, a history of underwhelming clinical results, and is now pivoting to a new, still-unproven platform with a cash burn problem that could spell trouble for the company if there are any further hiccups or strategic pivoting. Investors must wait until 2026 or potentially longer for any meaningful clinical data. In the meantime, catalysts are limited, and the stock remains under pressure. This gives us enough of a reason to remain on the sidelines.

data.title: AMD: The Bear Case Is Not Over
data.description:
My thesis

Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) stock is currently almost 13% cheaper than it was when I shared my previous bearish call. The stock remains significantly overvalued despite the notable YTD share price weakness. There are several other bearish factors that support the bearish outlook. These include the uncertainty around tariffs and intensifying competitive pressure from Nvidia (NVDA). Due to all these negatives, I am confident that AMD remains a Strong Sell.

AMD stock analysis

There was some really unfavorable news for AMD recently, which is a report about Microsoft scrapping some data center plans. This is a notable red flag, because AI mania was the major reason behind AMD's outperformance against the broader stock market in 2023-2024. That said, the fact that the world's second-largest cloud provider might cut its data center spending is likely to be a strong bearish catalyst for the stock.

Apart from the potential downgrade in tech giants' data center spending plans, there is another big red flag called "Nvidia." According to analyst Blayne Curtis from Jefferies, "real-world throughput of NVDA’s H200 across a range of open-source models is substantially higher than AMD’s MI300x." From the forward-looking comments' perspective, the technological gap with Nvidia is "expected to widen." Having a technologically weaker product compared to your biggest rival is a significant fundamental flaw because it is impossible to exercise any pricing power with an inferior product.

Having a weaker pricing power is not just a short-term setback but will likely have its long-term adverse consequences for AMD. The gross margin comparison of NVDA and AMD suggests there is a massive gap of almost 25 percentage points, not basis points. Nvidia's gross margin recent pullback was due to the aggressive R&D spending boost, which will also likely play against AMD's long-term prospects. With weaker pricing power and less capacity to reinvest in growth and innovation, AMD is poised to lag far behind NVDA.

In addition, the competition risk is not only about Nvidia. Intel has been struggling over the past decade, but they recently appointed a new CEO who wants to refocus on innovation. I think that Intel is also a dangerous competitor for AMD because its R&D budget is almost three times larger, and it also has governmental support behind its back. Considering the fact that Intel has most of its manufacturing capacity facilities in the U.S. and President Trump's plans to "Make America Great Again," there is a possibility that Intel might continue getting governmental support with the new White House administration as well. The potential renaissance of Intel is another fundamental risk for AMD.

Apart from company-specific risks, there are notable risks for the entire semiconductor industry. After two years of the global AI craze and early bold forecasts regarding its ability to create trillions in economic value, some new reports suggest that the positive effect might be modest over the next decade. For example, MTI Institute professor, Daron Acemoglu estimates that only 5% of tasks will be able to be profitably performed by AI within the next decade. In addition, BCG AI Radar suggests that only 25% of executives see significant value from AI. Such cautious AI-related reports might lead to significant revenue growth downgrades for NVDA and AMD, which will significantly undermine the stock market's sentiment on semiconductors.

The global geopolitical uncertainty might be another big risk for AMD. Apart from the tariffs-related uncertainty, which might disrupt global supply chains, there is another large geopolitical risk for AMD. As we all remember, AMD's chips are mostly manufactured in Taiwan. This country has its long-lasting territorial disputes with China, and there are opinions saying that China might even try to invade Taiwan. Geopolitical tensions continue to rise as recent reports suggest that China will intensify its drills in the waters and airspace around Taiwan. The China-Taiwan situation getting more complicated is another red flag for AMD's investors.

Intrinsic value calculation

The most suitable discount rate to value AMD is cost of equity as AMD's total debt is low compared to the company's market cap. According to the CAPM approach, AMD's cost of equity is 11.7%.

The company's TTM-based FCF margin is 13.6%, which is insignificantly higher compared to the last five years' average. In addition, there is fierce competition from Nvidia that will highly likely put pressure on AMD's margins. Therefore, a notable FCF margin expansion is quite unlikely, and I implement the same value for all five years of my DCF. Wall Street analysts forecast a notable revenue growth deceleration between FY 2026-2027, and I continue this trend for the remainder of my DCF's horizon. I expect years 3-5 revenue growth to decelerate by 200-bps each. Due to the increasing level of uncertainty and intensifying competitive pressure from Nvidia, I downgrade the perpetual growth rate from 5% to 4%.

AMD's intrinsic value per share is $83, 19% lower than the last close. That said, even after a massive share price drop and a quite aggressive 4% perpetual growth rate, AMD remains substantially overvalued.

What can go wrong with my thesis?

There are bullish factors as well. Some of them might work against my bearish thesis. For a quite popular stock like AMD, its current 14-Day RSI indicator of below 50 might be considered as a large buying signal. In addition, the stock still trades above $100 per share, which might be a very strong support level.

Furthermore, the analysts' sentiment around AMD remains quite bullish. The average rating from Seeking Alpha authors and Wall Street analysts is Buy. The vast majority of Wall Street ratings represent Strong Buy. In addition, the number of bearish ratings is quite low, meaning that other authors and experts mostly do not share my pessimism. This might also be a bullish signal for investors to buy the dip.

From the business perspective, Nvidia's current dominance does not guarantee that it will be ahead of AMD forever. The technological landscape is aggressively evolving, which might create an opportunity window for AMD. Moreover, the company already has experience of successfully competing in the CPU field against Intel.

Summary

I think that all the negatives by far outweigh the positives. Moreover, the stock is significantly overvalued. This makes me reiterate a Strong Sell rating for AMD.

data.title: Calamos Investment Team Outlooks, April 2025
data.description:
By John P. Calamos

Markets hate uncertainty, so it’s not surprising that the first quarter was a turbulent one. Since President Trump’s election in November, investors have been impatient for certainty about how policies will influence individual companies, sectors, and the economy. In particular, confusion about the administration’s tariff policy and its economic impacts have been a focal point of investor apprehension.

As we have noted throughout the years, fiscal policy is the foundation of a country’s economic success. The pillars of the administration’s platform—downsizing the government, decreasing regulation, substantially reducing the federal debt, implementing tariffs to bolster government revenue and US economic activity, and immigration policy—are expansive and would usher in a new chapter for the US economy.

Implementing change at this scale cannot happen overnight, and we encourage investors to remember that the transition will take time. Long-term gain rarely comes without short-term pain.

In the meantime, I encourage investors to take a step back and think long term. I’m always thinking about what the history of the markets can teach us, but that’s been especially true lately. Over the past year, I’ve been engaged in a special, once-in-a-lifetime project: my soon-to-be-published biography, The Sky’s the Limit: Lessons in Service, Entrepreneurship and Achieving the American Dream. This process has led me to reflect even more deeply on the lessons of the market and on my own experiences as an investor. There have been many corrections and crashes, and periods of profound economic and social uncertainty.

For investors who are feeling uncertain about what may or may not be on the horizon, I would offer this: The economy and markets are always changing—and often, the changes are monumental and unprecedented when they are happening. Once the change is in the rear-view mirror, investors often forget how acute the uncertainty was, so in the face of the next round of change, it’s easy to forget how the economy has adapted, how companies have adjusted to disruptions, and how new investment opportunities emerged.

Fifty-plus years as an investor has taught me that the economy is resilient, and the seeds of long-term opportunity are always being sown. As you will read in the commentaries that follow, our investment teams agree. We’ll be adding to our library in the coming days, as well.

A Distinctly Discriminating Turmoil

By Michael Grant

Commentary around financial markets can confuse catalysts with causes. Many point to Trump’s tariff wars as one source of the recent turmoil across US equities. It seems clear that many underestimated the commitment of the resurgent Trump presidency to radical change. How else to explain why the consensus outlook for 2025 has not survived the first quarter?

The more genuine mistake has been to underestimate Trump’s willingness to overlook the turbulence that the pursuit of his agenda would entail. In his own words, “Markets are going to go up and they’re going to go down but, you know what, we have to rebuild our country.” Investors are discovering there is no “Trump put” under equities.

The distribution of the latest turmoil reveals the essential problem: the overvaluation of, over-optimism about, and over-exposure to America’s largest-cap growth stocks. The collapse of the momentum factor in March—one of the fastest unwinds in 40 years1—highlights the danger of overstaying the party, or “dancing as long as the music plays.”2

This positioning crisis across the investment industry has symmetrical logic. The counterpart of everyone’s overweight to US growth equities has been a corresponding underweight outside of America and to cyclical value in particular. The comparative resilience of equities in Europe, Canada, and Mexico—the targets of Trump’s tariffs—is telling.

Again, the catalysts are less important than the underlying diagnosis. The obsession with Trump generates the impression that his pronouncements are the most significant influence. In our view, it is an accident of history that Trump’s second presidency coincided with the moment when America’s growth equity assets had attained unsustainable levels.

That said, Trumpism is the shock therapy for Europe’s political establishment, succeeding where even the invasion of Ukraine failed. His direct engagement with Russia has forced Europe to confront the consequences of its decline: irrelevance on every world stage. One of several expressions3 of this panic is the discriminating resilience of European equities.

Trump’s initiatives are succeeding where 50 years of mainstream policy recommendations have failed: they are producing a degree of rebalancing of international demand. Demand in parts of Europe must revive from a position of stagnation. The pace and timing of this rebalancing will be decisive for the cyclical value industries that dominate non-US markets.

Tariffs, the growth scare, and the bursting of the AI bubble—all have coalesced to produce signs of hesitation in US consumer demand. Here, there are parallels with the Silicon Valley Bank liquidity crisis in spring 2023, when economic fears intensified but abated quickly. Today as then, labor markets appear firm, consumer balance sheets remain pristine, and real wage growth is positive.

We view the coming changes in trade and immigration as less impactful for the US economy than those related to regulation and fiscal policies. The latter are positive supply-side shocks. The consequence of limiting fiscal activity is a complicated story, with some short-term pain that we interpret as slowdown, not recession.

Fortuitously, the message of market behavior is that US recession risk is low for now. Bond yields have only declined moderately, credits spreads have not moved decisively, commodity prices are resilient, and cyclical sectors have not underperformed. The implication is a severe correction of equity values, not a bear market.

Although today’s headlines diminish our confidence, we continue to see expansion as the most likely path ahead because the key drivers of recession are not yet in place. Sentiment matters, but fundamentals matter more. Higher uncertainty is not the same as the certainty of recession. Of course, all of this bears monitoring.

Volatility in a Very Broad Range

Entering 2025, we anticipated two scenarios for US equities: volatility or a bear market. By March, equities stood at the threshold of a bear market. However, it is too early for this because the US economy is confronting a slowdown, not a recession. This favors the scenario of volatility rather than outright bear—for now.

The US equity sell-off has been held at the important support of ~5500 for the S&P 500 Index. This zone may be retested, if only because further tariff salvos are coming, and many investors will sell into any revival. At worst, we see downside to 5300, which is consistent with the interpretation of “correction with high volatility.” A wide trading range for US benchmarks should be assumed; our portfolio is hedged accordingly.

Once this phase of the shakeout is complete, US equities could return to their 2025 year highs by summer on the assumption of stabilization in the economic data. We expect the Federal Reserve to deliver a reduction in the federal funds rate of at least 50 basis points in the May-to-August period. In other words, we anticipate a setting of three steps forward and two steps back for US equities in Q2.

What happens after will be strongly influenced by the correctness of our structural perspective, which we described last autumn as the “Invincibility Syndrome.”4 While a degree of investors’ over-optimism has been removed, the obstacle of over-ownership is unresolved. Investors are likely to reduce risk into any revival, which implies recovery cannot be V-shaped.

The riposte to the Invincibility Syndrome and its implications for prolonged disturbance is this: has the world that produced the decade-plus outperformance of US growth equities really changed that much? After all, the attributes of these businesses are substantial in terms of profitability and security. Could this not simply be a temporary setback rather than a more strategic turning point?

Dogmatism about the future is rarely a productive investing quality. And yet, two arguments stand tall over the question of sustained American financial supremacy. First, asset valuations matter, at least when they are extreme and especially when they are backed by considerations of sentiment and positioning. The comparison with the year 2000 can be qualified, but not entirely dismissed.

Second, the exceptionalism of US financial markets was enabled by America’s ability to attract capital for competitive returns and security from all parts of the world. Today, few doubt the narrative of a more fragmented world economy with greater restrictions on trade and investment. Disruption implies that capital can go home, which the latest investor flows highlight has begun.

In this version of the new global order, it is hard to believe that financial flows will not be affected in some fashion. While the US economy is less vulnerable to Trump’s policies than those of its major trading partners, the unintended and indirect effects upon flows of portfolio capital may be more disruptive than many can imagine today.

This is why the first quarter might be judged as an overture for what is to come.
